---
title: "Repropducible workflow"
---

{{< include ../_hardcoded_values.qmd >}}

## Fundamentals

To achieve a maximal reproducible workflow one can use **Version control**. Version control is the systematic process of documenting every change made to your scripts, data, or outputs. It allows you to trace **when**, **why**, and **by whom** specific changes were made. Most importantly, it acts as a safety net, allowing you to restore previous versions of your work if something goes wrong.

## GitHub

[**GitHub**](https://github.com/) is essentially a specialized cloud provider—similar to Google Drive or OneDrive—but designed specifically for code and version control. In GitHub, projects are organized into **repositories**, which function as project folders containing all your files and their history.

By using the [**GitHub Desktop application**](https://desktop.github.com/download/), you can sync a folder on your local device with a repository on GitHub. Your progress is tracked through commits. Unlike a standard 'save,' a commit creates a permanent snapshot of your project at a specific point in time. Instead of overwriting files, Git records the precise differences between versions.

Once you have committed your changes locally, you can 'upload' them to the cloud using a **push**. Conversely, if the repository has been updated (e.g., by a collaborator or your supervisor), you can download those updates using a **pull**. Always pull before you start working and push once you finish a task to keep everyone in sync.

::: callout-note
As a student of the University of Bern you are eligible for a free GitHub Pro account, which gives you access to the GitHub Copilot and other benefits. For more information check out [GitHubs information for students](https://github.com/education/students).
:::

## Git

While GitHub Desktop is a great starting point, the true power lies in the underlying software called [**Git**](https://git-scm.com/). Git is the 'engine' that powers both GitHub and the Desktop app. After a one-time setup, you can connect Git directly to your programming environment (like RStudio). This allows you to perform commits, pushes, and pulls directly within the same window where you write your code, removing the need to switch back and forth between different applications. This integration streamlines your workflow and makes versioning a seamless part of your coding habit.

::: callout-note
You can find instructions for the one-time setup of Git, GitHub, R, and the connection between these [here](https://happygitwithr.com/).
:::

::: callout-note
This website itself was build with the open source programming languages of R, Python, the IDE of RStudio, Git and GitHub for version control. You can find the corresponding repository [here][repo-url].
:::

## .gitignore

A `.gitignore` file prevents Git from tracking specific files or folders, ensuring they are never uploaded to GitHub. To use it, create a text file named `.gitignore` in your project root and list any files or patterns (e.g., *.csv, .Rhistory) you wish to exclude. This is a critical security step for protecting sensitive participant data and maintaining a clean repository by excluding unnecessary large files which could break the upload.

::: callout-note
Note that on the [GitHub repository][repo-url] you can find the respective .gitignore-file for this website project. You can use it as a template for your own project and modify it according to your needs.
:::

## renv

**renv** (Reproducible Environments) records the exact versions of all R packages you are using. This ensures that anyone who wants to reproduce your work can simply install the same package versions, preventing issues that arise from updates or changes in package behavior. By using renv, you can create a snapshot of your project's dependencies, making it easier to share and collaborate on your code while maintaining reproducibility.

You initialize `renv` in your project by running `renv::init()` in the R console. This will create a `renv.lock` file that contains all the package versions you are using. Whenever you install a new package or update an existing one, make sure to run `renv::snapshot()` to update the lock file. When someone else wants to reproduce your work, they can run `renv::restore()` to install the exact package versions specified in the lock file.

::: callout-note
It is also possible to combine .gitignore and renv by adding the `renv/library/` folder to the .gitignore file. This way, the actual package files are not tracked by Git, but the `renv.lock` file is still included in the repository, allowing others to restore the same environment without uploading large package files.
:::
